topic: The "Ghost" Economy of Recycled Cooking Oil in Lagos
definition: a small, representative system that reflects the characteristics of a larger whole
example: The Lagos cooking oil trade is a microcosm of the entire informal economy.

topic: The "Ghost" Economy of Recycled Cooking Oil in Lagos
definition: not controlled or supervised by official rules or laws
example: The market for recycled cooking oil is almost entirely unregulated.

topic: The "Ghost" Economy of Recycled Cooking Oil in Lagos
definition: a raw material or primary agricultural product that can be bought and sold
example: Used cooking oil has become a valuable commodity in the informal sector.

topic: The "Ghost" Economy of Recycled Cooking Oil in Lagos
definition: a factor that motivates or encourages someone to do something
example: The price difference between fresh and recycled oil is the main incentive for vendors.

topic: The "Ghost" Economy of Recycled Cooking Oil in Lagos
definition: spread out over a large area; not concentrated
example: The market is so diffuse that any single enforcement action has little impact.

topic: The "Ghost" Economy of Recycled Cooking Oil in Lagos
definition: a substance used to adulterate (make impure) another substance
example: Mineral oil is a common adulterant added to recycled cooking oil to increase volume.

topic: The "Ghost" Economy of Recycled Cooking Oil in Lagos
definition: the ability to track the history, location, or application of a product
example: Biodiesel production requires full traceability from restaurant to refinery.

topic: The "Ghost" Economy of Recycled Cooking Oil in Lagos
definition: a very small difference between cost and revenue, leaving little room for error
example: Street vendors operate on razor-thin margins, so every cent saved on oil matters.

topic: The "Ghost" Economy of Recycled Cooking Oil in Lagos
definition: the unintended or unauthorized flow of something from one system to another
example: Even formal restaurants have leakage of used oil into the informal market.

topic: The "Ghost" Economy of Recycled Cooking Oil in Lagos
definition: the process by which one thing takes in or absorbs another
example: The health costs of bad oil are absorbed by the poorest consumers.

topic: The Nuclear Option: Why Game Theory Says a Losing Player Might Choose to End the Game for Everyone
definition: A threat that the target believes will actually be carried out.
example: For a "poison pill" to work, it must be a credible threat that the board will actually enact it.

topic: The Nuclear Option: Why Game Theory Says a Losing Player Might Choose to End the Game for Everyone
definition: A scenario where one party holds something valuable (or threatens destruction) to force the other party's compliance.
example: The losing player created a hostage situation by threatening to ruin the game for everyone.

topic: The Nuclear Option: Why Game Theory Says a Losing Player Might Choose to End the Game for Everyone
definition: A situation where a player cannot credibly commit to a future course of action, leading to a suboptimal outcome.
example: The lack of a credible commitment mechanism made the peace treaty fragile.

topic: The Nuclear Option: Why Game Theory Says a Losing Player Might Choose to End the Game for Everyone
definition: A desire to hurt, annoy, or offend someone, even at one's own cost.
example: The player's decision to feed the enemy team was driven by pure spite, not a desire to win.

topic: The Nuclear Option: Why Game Theory Says a Losing Player Might Choose to End the Game for Everyone
definition: A stable state in a game where no player has an incentive to change their strategy unilaterally.
example: The "nuclear option" often destroys the normal equilibrium of a game.

topic: The Nuclear Option: Why Game Theory Says a Losing Player Might Choose to End the Game for Everyone
definition: Done before an opponent can act, in order to gain an advantage or prevent something.
example: The winning player considered a preemptive strike to disable the losing player's sabotage option.

topic: The Nuclear Option: Why Game Theory Says a Losing Player Might Choose to End the Game for Everyone
definition: To pretend to have a stronger position or a more credible threat than one actually has.
example: The general tried to bluff, but the enemy called his threat a lie.

topic: The Nuclear Option: Why Game Theory Says a Losing Player Might Choose to End the Game for Everyone
definition: A sequence of actions where each party's defensive move is seen as an offensive threat by the other, leading to increasingly dangerous counteractions.
example: The trade war tariffs created an escalation spiral that hurt both economies.

topic: The Nuclear Option: Why Game Theory Says a Losing Player Might Choose to End the Game for Everyone
definition: To yield or give up something, often in a negotiation.
example: The losing player only agreed to concede after extracting a promise of a rematch.

topic: The Nuclear Option: Why Game Theory Says a Losing Player Might Choose to End the Game for Everyone
definition: A doctrine of military strategy where full-scale use of nuclear weapons by two or more opposing sides would cause the complete annihilation of both the attacker and the defender.
example: The Cold War was balanced on the terrifying logic of mutually assured destruction.

topic: The Invisible Infrastructure: How Sea Lanes Shape Global Trade
definition: a regularly used navigable route for large vessels on oceans or large lakes
example: The Strait of Malacca is the most congested sea lane in the world.

topic: The Invisible Infrastructure: How Sea Lanes Shape Global Trade
definition: the total weight a ship can safely carry, including cargo, fuel, and crew
example: Greek-owned vessels had a total deadweight tonnage of 834 million tons in 2018.

topic: The Invisible Infrastructure: How Sea Lanes Shape Global Trade
definition: a narrow, strategic waterway that is vulnerable to disruption
example: The Suez Canal is a critical chokepoint for global oil shipments.

topic: The Invisible Infrastructure: How Sea Lanes Shape Global Trade
definition: relating to the sea, especially in terms of navigation or commerce
example: Greece has a long maritime tradition dating back to ancient times.

topic: The Invisible Infrastructure: How Sea Lanes Shape Global Trade
definition: a ship designed to transport loose cargo like grain, ore, or coal
example: Greek shipping families dominate the global fleet of tankers and bulk carriers.

topic: The Invisible Infrastructure: How Sea Lanes Shape Global Trade
definition: deep and wide enough for ships to pass through
example: A waterway must be navigable for maritime transport to be feasible.

topic: The Invisible Infrastructure: How Sea Lanes Shape Global Trade
definition: the dominant wind direction in a region, historically crucial for sailing routes
example: During the Age of Sail, sea lanes were determined by prevailing winds.

topic: The Invisible Infrastructure: How Sea Lanes Shape Global Trade
definition: involving more than one mode of transport (e.g., ship and truck)
example: Containerization made intermodal shipping efficient and cheap.

topic: The Invisible Infrastructure: How Sea Lanes Shape Global Trade
definition: the amount by which a country's imports exceed its exports
example: Greek shipping receipts cover about one-third of the nation's trade deficit.

topic: The Invisible Infrastructure: How Sea Lanes Shape Global Trade
definition: a group of ships owned by a single company or nation
example: Greece controls the world's largest merchant fleet by tonnage.

topic: When the Government Is the Only Seller: The Mechanics of State Monopoly
definition: a market with a single supplier of a good or service
example: The government's monopoly on postal service means no private company can legally deliver letters.

topic: When the Government Is the Only Seller: The Mechanics of State Monopoly
definition: a monopoly created, owned, and operated by the government
example: A state monopoly on liquor sales gives the government control over pricing and distribution.

topic: When the Government Is the Only Seller: The Mechanics of State Monopoly
definition: a monopoly in which competition is legally prohibited
example: A coercive monopoly like the state-run lottery leaves citizens with no alternative provider.

topic: When the Government Is the Only Seller: The Mechanics of State Monopoly
definition: the ability of a firm to raise prices above competitive levels
example: A state monopoly has significant market power, but may choose not to exercise it for political reasons.

topic: When the Government Is the Only Seller: The Mechanics of State Monopoly
definition: the cost of producing one additional unit of a good
example: A monopoly can set a price well above marginal cost, earning monopoly profit.

topic: When the Government Is the Only Seller: The Mechanics of State Monopoly
definition: a monopoly given by the state to a private entity, such as a patent
example: A patent is a government-granted monopoly that incentivizes innovation.

topic: When the Government Is the Only Seller: The Mechanics of State Monopoly
definition: a monopoly that arises because high fixed costs make single-provider operation most efficient
example: Water utilities are often natural monopolies because duplicating pipes is wasteful.

topic: When the Government Is the Only Seller: The Mechanics of State Monopoly
definition: a market with a single buyer
example: The government acts as a monopsony when it is the only purchaser of military aircraft.

topic: When the Government Is the Only Seller: The Mechanics of State Monopoly
definition: to use profits from one product or market to cover losses in another
example: The state monopoly may cross-subsidize rural mail delivery with urban profits.

topic: When the Government Is the Only Seller: The Mechanics of State Monopoly
definition: relating to laws that prevent monopolies and promote competition
example: Antitrust laws can break up private monopolies but do not apply to state monopolies.

topic: When the Government Is the Only Seller: The Mechanics of State Monopoly
definition: obstacles that make it difficult for new firms to enter a market
example: Legal barriers to entry are the strongest form, as seen in state monopolies.

topic: When the Government Is the Only Seller: The Mechanics of State Monopoly
definition: a market dominated by a small number of sellers
example: Unlike a monopoly, an oligopoly has several firms that may collude or compete.
